# 0012. Evidence is generated by the pipeline, not maintained by hand

Date: 2026-06

## Status

Accepted

## Context

In regulated environments the recurring failure mode is not missing controls — it is documents that describe an imagined system. A spreadsheet says every service has an owner and a runbook; three reorgs later, half the rows are fiction, and nobody finds out until an audit or an incident. Hand-maintained evidence decays silently because nothing breaks when it goes stale.

This platform makes claims a reviewer should be able to check: every service has an owner and tier, critical services have runbooks, images are scanned and signed, workloads are hardened, infrastructure code meets a policy baseline. The question is what form the proof takes.

## Decision

Evidence is a build artifact. Every claim above is produced — and enforced — by the pipeline, so the claim cannot drift from reality without CI going red:

- Catalog consistency is a CI gate. `platform validate` cross-checks `catalog/` against the repo tree: a service directory without a catalog entry fails; an entry without deploy manifests fails ("would never deploy"); t1/t2 entries require `links.runbook` pointing at a file that exists, t1 additionally requires `links.dashboard`. The rendered portal page (`platform catalog render`) is derived from the same validated data, so the "service inventory" document is regenerated on every run instead of curated.
- Images carry their own evidence. Service CI ([reusable-service-ci.yml](../../.github/workflows/reusable-service-ci.yml)) gates on Trivy (fail on CRITICAL/HIGH) before pushing; [release.yml](../../.github/workflows/release.yml) promotes the PR-tested digest without rebuilding, signs it with cosign keyless (GitHub OIDC), and attaches a syft SBOM. "What is in this image and who built it" is answered by the registry, not a wiki.
- Manifests pass policies that are themselves unit-tested. Rendered kustomize output goes through kubeconform and conftest against [policy/kubernetes/workloads.rego](../../policy/kubernetes/workloads.rego) — missing limits/requests, non-root violations, missing `drop ALL`, writable root filesystems, `:latest`, foreign registries all deny. The policies have their own test suite run via `conftest verify`, so the control is verified, not just present.
- Terraform passes checkov as a hard gate across both providers and both environments ([terraform.yml](../../.github/workflows/terraform.yml)).

The principle: the artifact IS the evidence. A green pipeline run on a commit is a machine-checked statement about that commit, timestamped and reproducible. Generated evidence cannot drift from reality, because it is recomputed from reality on every change.

## Alternatives considered

Maintained compliance matrices — a controls spreadsheet or "security posture" document mapping claims to implementations, updated by whoever remembers. This is the incumbent in most regulated shops and it lost on its own track record: it drifts the moment the code moves, it doubles the cost of every change (do the work, then update the matrix), and at audit time it produces the worst outcome — confident documentation contradicted by the system, which reads as either negligence or deception. A matrix also cannot fail a merge; a gate can.

## Consequences

- Evidence quality is bounded by gate quality. A weak policy generates weak evidence with high confidence: if `workloads.rego` forgot to check `allowPrivilegeEscalation`, every pipeline would still go green and the green would be misleading. Generated evidence removes drift, not blind spots — so the gates themselves need a review cadence, and their unit tests (`workloads_test.rego`, the CLI validation tests, the triage fixtures) only cover what someone thought to encode.
- Some evidence genuinely resists generation: threat models, architectural rationale, scope decisions. Those remain prose (this ADR series), and they carry exactly the drift risk this decision eliminates elsewhere.
- Auditors and reviewers must be pointed at pipelines and registries rather than handed a single document. The catalog render mitigates this, but "the evidence is in CI" demands more from the reader than a spreadsheet does.
